speaker: interviewer
text: Slate 5. October 26, 1995. We're speaking with Mr. Sam Zelikson. I'm Leslie Fass. Mr. Zelikson, you were talking about your experiences in America working for a butcher. And then you weren't paid by him, and you put him-- you hung him on--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the hook you put the meat on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were being brought by the policeman to his friend, the judge.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Friday night. So his wife says, let's eat supper before anything. I know you didn't eat. I told you I didn't eat. And I started to tell the judge how I worked, and she didn't even let me go for lunch. She gave me a half bagel or a piece of bread with coffee. In 10 minutes, she said, that's it. You take 10 minutes for lunch. And I told the judge. And the woman, his wife, cried. I told her. And the judge says to [INAUDIBLE], bring him over right away, and tell him to bring the check book. And the judge figured out that he's supposed to pay me $0.75 an hour plus time and a half. I was supposed to only work eight hours plus an hour lunch, which I never had. To make a long story short, he made out a check for me for $1,000. The judge fined him $5,000 for [? exploit ?] labor. And he started to scream to the judge, he hasn't got enough money. So the judge says, let me see the check book. And he looked at the check book and said, you have enough for another $5,000 fine. And I told all the women what happened with the chicken, and another butcher opened up about two blocks away. And everybody went to the other butcher. They couldn't believe it, what a Jewish man could do to me. And he had to close up his whole business. I went back to HIAS and I told them I have no more job with the butcher. See what they can do. I saw a friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says to me, you like to learn glass? I says, anything. I'll do anything. He says, I have a friend of mine. He owns a small store, a glass store. Go talk to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in and I says to him, Ruby. His name-- Ruby August.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he sent me. [INAUDIBLE] sent me over. And he says he knows you, and you need a worker. And I'm a hard worker. He says, are you afraid to cut glass? I says, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as soon as he showed me how to cut the glass, the glass slipped and I cut my thumb. And he got excited. I says, give me just a piece of rag. I'll put it around and I'll do my work.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, no, I have to take you to the hospital. I said, for what? It's only a cut. He said, no, you need stitches. I didn't even know what stitches meant. I couldn't speak a word English.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I worked for him for three years. But after I worked for him a couple months, I says, I have to learn English. And I am going to go learn English. I'm going to go in school, night school. So I worked for him from 7 o'clock in the morning to 6 o'clock at night, and 7:00 to 11 o'clock, I went to night school. And thank God I learned English. I worked for him for three years, and I says to him, Ruby, I can't make enough money. I'm thinking to look for a girl. Maybe get married, have a family. I want to become a partner.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, wow, you want to become partner? I said, yeah, I want to become partner. He said, how much money you got? I says, I got reparations from Germany-- $5,000. That's all I have in the bank. He said, oh, that's good enough. $5,000 was, at that time, a lot of money. How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, at that time, about 19. 19 or 20, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I give him the money. He says, give me the money. I said, wait a moment. I've been through a lot, and I know what's all about. I'll give you the money, then we make an agreement. He said, what kind of agreement? I says, a contract. He says, no, I don't write a contract. We just say the word and that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, first of all, I worked for you for three years. You haven't got a truck. You can't transport the glass with cars. We used to transport the glass with a car because we had a 1936 Plymouth. Had a running board. We used to put the glass on a running board, and people thought I was crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I says to him, we'll buy a truck. He says, OK, we'll buy a truck. He says, but where are you going to get the money? I says, from the $5,000. And we'll hire another guy. He called me all kind of names, that I dictate him. He told [INAUDIBLE] you [NON-ENGLISH] he told me. You tell me what to do? I said, yeah, I'm telling you if you want my $5,000.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we worked quite a long time partners, and we made pretty good. I met my wife. And before I got married, I was doing a job on the second floor, and I fell out through the window and I broke my leg. And I says to my wife-- at that time she was my girl-- I said, now we can't get married. She said, over my dead body. And we went to the hospital. The Rabbi was there. So I says to the Rabbi, I can't get married now. I have to postpone. He says, no, not in Jewish religion. You get married with a broken leg [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] And I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was 1956. I got married. I even drove to Niagara Falls with my broken leg. And I danced on the floor with my-- I had a cast up to my thigh. All the way. It weighed about 35 pounds. But I didn't give in. And thank God I had a wonderful-- I met a wonderful wife. And I had five kids. And my son got sick. And I didn't know what it was. And in middle of the night, he ran fever, 104 fever. I called a doctor. The doctor couldn't come. He sent in another doctor. The doctor says, oh, he has bronchial fever, whatever [? he ?] called it. He says, I'll give him an antibiotic and he'll be all right. And for a couple of days, he was all right, and then it started in again. So at that time, my doctor came. It was also at night. And he says, we got to take him direct to the hospital because he has double pneumonia, and I think one of his lungs is collapsed. We took him to the hospital. And it took about three weeks, and he passed away. I lost my son. Like, the whole world fell apart. And I kept on working so hard. I didn't care what time it was. Not to forget, but my mind should be occupied. I couldn't get over it. And then I went out on a job. And I did in Riverdale, in Jewish Center. And I went up on the ladder, 18 feet, to put in a glass. And I just told my guy, I says, stay by the ladder and hold it while I go up and put in the glass. As soon as I put in the glass, a girl passed by with a bikini, and he turned around and let go of the ladder to look at the girl, and I fell down and broke both legs in six places. I broke both ankles and both heels. And I had three specialists. And one guy says, forget about walking. The next guy says, maybe 10, 15 years you'll be able to walk. And the third says, it's up to you. If you're strong enough, you could take pain, and you're not squeamish, you'll walk. And it took 11 months, and I walked. And they couldn't believe it. They told me I should go on the-- at that time it was a television show they had, I think. They says I should go and tell them the story there. And that was-- after I came out from the hospital, I did another job on 14th street with my partner's brother. And I told him not to keep the glass too high, and not to hit against the iron. And he was strong, a 350-pounder. And we picked up the glass and he hit the iron, and it cut my hand right off. And they had to put it in a basket, an ice basket. He passed out. Two women in the store passed out. I had to call my own ambulance.

speaker: interviewer
text: I put a wire around my arm, tightened it, and I put a rag. And I'm sitting on the chair. I called. The ambulance came. And he says, who's sick? He says, you must be kidding. He saw them laying on the floor. I says, look, the blood is coming from my arm and my shoulder cut. And he saw there was a basket with ice, and he grabbed me. He says, can you go? I says, I don't know, I'll try.

speaker: interviewee
text: And soon I tried, I fell. He put the oxygen mask on me and took me to Bellevue Hospital. That was Dr. [? Chu, ?] the same doctor that operated on [INAUDIBLE]. He operated on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, what do you want, a local anesthetic? I said, whatever you do, I want to see. He says, that's going to be a local. He says, the operation will take between three to four hours. He says, I'll give you a local for four hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: He gave me the local. The operation took 10 and a half hours. They had to take a laser beam inside my hand to take the veins, because all the veins were cut. To pull the veins and sew them together. So inside, I must have had about 120 stitches. And he told me-- he says, he's terribly sorry that he underestimated the operation. And he can't give me one anesthetic on top of another. So I have to bear with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me a sack, a wool sack, to keep between my teeth and bear. And it took six and a half hours without an anesthetic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he kept on doing. Thank God my arm is not too bad. I can't close it, but I still have strength in it. That was my second. In 1991, I went from lunch to go back to the store, and it was raining. As I crossed the street, the light was with me. A woman, she was in a hurry to go pick up her child. She passed the red light and she hit me, and I flew up 20 feet in the air. And police was right near there, one policeman. I was out. My partner told me that. One policeman checked me. He says, forget about it. Call the morgue. He's gone. And the other policeman says, it happened before. Let me check him. And he put his finger on my neck, and he put a little mirror on my nose. That's what my partner told me. And he says, no, he's still breathing. And they took me to Bellevue Hospital. And I was there a couple of weeks. And in Bellevue, Hospital the patient next to me, he says, Sam, you know, today is the lotto-- $90 million. I says, oh, $90 million. I'm going to play. And I was saying my prayer. I always say not too loud. I was saying [INAUDIBLE]. And I felt a hand push me. And it felt, like, my son give him a signal. And my daughter was supposed to come that day-- it was Saturday-- to take me home. And I said to her, we're going to play the lottery. She says, Dad, you know, first let me take you home. She took me home. I says, let's play the lotto. Let's pick numbers. And we picked some numbers. And she said, Dad, I haven't got money. I took out $16-- don't ask me why-- and I give her $16. It was [INAUDIBLE]. They went downstairs and played the lotto. They came back with 16 tickets. And we were watching, 8 o'clock. They announced the lotto. And one number comes up, two numbers, the third, a fourth number. I says to my wife, hey, we have some money because four numbers, you get money. And then a fifth number. I said, oh, now we're in the clover. And then the sixth number. My kids start to jump and scream. I said listen, don't get excited. Let's call out the lotto and find out what's all about. And we called up the lotto, and they say, according your numbers, it's confirmed that you won $90 million.

speaker: interviewer
text: I turn around. I says to my wife, what the hell I'm going to do with $90 million? She said, don't worry about it. Let's first get the money. We'll see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the next day, nine people came up. So we got $10 million. So, of course, I divided it with my family, with my daughter. That's why we call Zelikson six because I had four daughters. I still have four daughters, me and my wife. And they picked us up with the limo, took us downtown. And had a speech. And they saw us. And I had this sign the ticket right away. And they told us within a month, we get.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Uncle Sam took a nice bite in it. He's taken away $153,000. We're supposed to get $476,000 plus change. So we got $342,000. And, of course, I contributed a lot. I bought bonds for Israel. And I did for [? a lot of ?] other organizations. And my kids gave some.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to Israel. And they took us to Israel. And we were, like, a VIP there. And then I find out in Israel that I have cousins. And I find out I had about eight cousins-- second cousin, third cousins, first cousin. And I find other people that were in the concentration camp with me, in Kaiserwald, in [INAUDIBLE] ghetto, and in Stutthof.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told them. They read in the Israel paper that I won. And there were a lot of people from my city. And I went to a restaurant and I said, listen. I need the restaurant for tonight. He said, you're crazy. I'm doing business. How can you do it? I says, how much you want for tonight? He says, oh, it got to cost you at least $1,500. I says to him, I'll tell you what I'll do. I'll give you $1,800, plus I'll give the waiters the tips. But I want the best. I have about 30, 35 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I invited everybody-- my cousins, my friends. And I invited them all to the restaurant. That was in Netanya. And we had a beautiful-- and then went to Hawaii. And I sold my house in Yonkers, and I bought a house here. And still, the most important thing to me-- I like to tell the young people what survival is all about, what life is all about-- the meaning. To know what happened, how it happened, and, God forbid, it should never happen. That is the most contribution I can do. I do it freely, without charge. I had speeches I had from Merrill Lynch. I had from the other insurance company. I had for the rotary club. And I'm glad to do anything I can help out. That's my main concern. Of course, my main concern is my family. And God bless my daughter. She's going to have twins in January. That's my first dividends, which every parent expects. And that's my [INAUDIBLE], my life story. I have a lot more, but unfortunately, it would take too long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. And now my children will come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is when I got married. It's me and my wife. My wife is Janice. And this was taken-- the picture-- in the Bronx. I was on Simpson--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tell us about this picture, Sam.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my adopted brother. He's in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin Kobeck.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken on my 60th birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Alice, and my son that passed away, Barry. Barry J. Zelikson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Yonkers. And that was in 1970.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the most happiest picture. This was taken in 1991, January the 26th, when we won the New York State lotto. This is me, my wife, and my four daughters. From left to right-- Linda Zelikson, Hellene, Alice, and Denise. And my wife, Janice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is when I held a speech in the rotary club in Mount Kisco, and I told them about the Holocaust, how i survived. And this was taken last year. Would you introduce your family, please? Yes. This is my lovely wife, and this is my lovely daughter, Denise, and this is my lovely daughter, Linda. That's part of my other two daughters, which at present are not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Linda, would you like to make a statement how you feel about your father being a survivor of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to make a statement about how you feel about your father being a survivor of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, I would. I'm very proud of him for coming forward and telling his story because I know it's very hard for a lot of survivors. And when we were growing up, my father really never shared it with us, so it was always some kind of this dark secret that you just didn't talk about because it was too painful. And we really never understood. And my father always taught us to believe in God and to believe in our religion. And he never-- with everything that he went through, he always believed in God, and he always believed in being a Jew and in being the person that he is. And he never gave up on that. And he installed that in all of us, and that was very important. But it really didn't connect with us until my father started opening up and telling us about his experiences. And then we understood. And we understood more about the pain and more about the tolerance and more about how to treat one another and more about being a Jew. And it really made a connection between the six million and all of those who survived and all of those who carry on and never forget. And it made it all seem like it was a connection to come together and to realize that those six million died for our freedom-- our freedom to be who we are, our freedom to be Jews. And that we should never forget that. And they're part of who we are, and our children will be part of who they, and part of who we are. And that's what I've learned from my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Denise, would you like to say something about your father being a survivor of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would. Growing up and being Jewish, you learn through the history of Jews that we're always persecuted. But, you know, you never felt a part of it. And I think my father being a survivor-- it brought it home. And when you sit at Passover and you read about the Exodus and the persecution of Jews, you understand more of having gone through that experience of knowing a survivor, and knowing how the Jews have suffered, and that it can happen. And that you should know that it will never happen again. And my father has taught us never to lose your faith. Be proud of who you are. And love everyone, no matter if they're Jewish or a Gentile or whatever. And he brought a strong faith and determination. And he is a very proud man. And I'm very proud to be his daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. [SIDE CONVERSATION]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my family portrait. My wife, my four daughters, and my son. My son, Barry, my daughter, the oldest daughter, Hellene, Alice, Denise, and Linda. And I'm very proud of it.
